question: How do I wire the Micro820 to the GS11?
ideal_answer: Three wires, shielded twisted pair: Micro820 D+ to GS11 SG+, Micro820 D- to GS11 SG-, Micro820 G to GS11 SGND. The RJ45 jack on the GS11 is NOT Ethernet — it is a multi-pin serial connector; use the screw-terminal RS-485 connections instead. Terminate the cable shield at one end only (drive end preferred). Keep the RS-485 run separated from VFD motor leads to avoid EMI; 120-ohm termination across D+/D- at the far end is recommended for runs over 10 ft.
contexts: Micro820 D+ goes to GS11 SG+, D- goes to SG-, G goes to SGND. RS-485 is a differential pair — polarity matters and a swap fails silently with no fault.|The GS11 RJ45 jack on the front is NOT Ethernet — it is a multi-pin serial connector. Plugging a CAT5 cable into a PC NIC or switch will not work. Use the screw-terminal RS-485 wiring instead.|Use shielded twisted pair, terminate shield at one end only (drive end preferred), keep RS-485 separate from VFD motor leads, add a 120-ohm termination resistor across D+/D- at the far end for long runs.

question: What register do I write to run the drive forward?
ideal_answer: Write decimal 18 (hex 0x12) to GS11 holding register 8192 (hex 0x2000) using Modbus function code 06 (Write Single Register). Register 8192 is the run/stop/direction command word: 1 = stop, 18 = run forward, 20 = run reverse. Before writing the run command, make sure you have already written the desired frequency (Hz x 100) to register 8193 — for example 3000 for 30 Hz — otherwise the drive will start at whatever the previous reference was.
contexts: GS11 Modbus register 8192 (0x2000) is the run/stop/direction command. Values: 1 = stop, 18 = run forward, 20 = run reverse. Use function code 06 (Write Single Register) or 16 (Write Multiple) from the master.|Standard sequence: first write Hz*100 to register 8193 (frequency reference), then write 18 to register 8192 to start forward. Writing the run command before setting frequency starts the drive at the last reference, which may be unsafe.|Function code 06 (Write Single Holding Register) is sufficient for both 8192 and 8193. FC16 (Write Multiple) lets you write both registers in one transaction, avoiding the race where RUN lands before the new frequency.

question: Drive won't respond to Modbus
ideal_answer: Work the symptom list in order. (1) Verify P00.21 = 2 so the drive accepts the run command from RS-485; if it is 0 or 1 the drive ignores writes to register 8192. (2) Verify P00.20 = 1 so the frequency reference comes from RS-485; otherwise the drive starts but at the keypad's 0 Hz and won't move. (3) Match all four serial settings end-to-end: baud (P09.01 ↔ CCW baud), parity and stop bits (P09.04 frame code ↔ CCW parity + stop), data bits = 8, and slave ID (P09.00 ↔ slave ID in the MSG instruction). (4) Confirm D+ and D- are not swapped at either end — RS-485 polarity is silent when wrong, no fault on the drive. (5) Bond Micro820 G to GS11 SGND. (6) Power-cycle the drive after any P09 change; serial port only re-initializes on reboot.
contexts: Drive won't respond — first check P00.21 (must = 2 for run from RS-485) and P00.20 (must = 1 for freq from RS-485). If only one is set, drive starts but sits at 0 Hz or never starts at all.|RS-485 polarity failures are silent: swapped D+/D- produces no fault and no answer. Swap one end first when comms time out. Also verify G to SGND bonding.|Serial mismatch checklist: baud (P09.01 vs CCW), parity + stop bits (P09.04 frame code vs CCW), slave ID (P09.00 vs MSG slave). Mismatched parity looks identical to wiring break — Micro820 just logs timeouts.|GS11 P09 parameter changes save immediately but the serial port keeps running on the old config until power cycle. Cycle 24VDC or main power after any P09 edit.

question: What value for 30 Hz?
ideal_answer: Write 3000 to register 8193. The GS11 frequency reference register is scaled as Hz × 100, so 30.00 Hz = 3000, 10.00 Hz = 1000, 60.00 Hz = 6000. Writing 30 instead of 3000 sets the drive to 0.30 Hz, which looks like the drive is stalled or growling at very low speed — this is one of the most common bugs when first commissioning Modbus control.
contexts: GS11 register 8193 (0x2001) is the frequency reference, scaled Hz × 100, unsigned 16-bit. 30.00 Hz = 3000, 60.00 Hz = 6000, 10.00 Hz = 1000.|Common mistake: sending 30 instead of 3000 for 30 Hz. The drive runs at 0.30 Hz — appears stuck or makes low-frequency growl noises. Always multiply Hz by 100.|P01.00 (max output frequency) clamps the value in register 8193. If P01.00 = 6000 (60 Hz) and you write 9000, the drive runs at 60 Hz with no fault. Increase P01.00 first if you need to exceed 60 Hz.

question: What are the modbus parameters for the word that I need to write on the GS11 drive
ideal_answer: The Modbus command word for the GS11 is register 8192 (hex 0x2000) — write decimal 18 to run forward, 20 to run reverse, 1 to stop, using function code 06 (Write Single Register). Before writing the run command, set the frequency reference at register 8193 (Hz × 100, so 3000 = 30 Hz) and confirm P00.21 = 2 (run source = RS-485) and P00.20 = 1 (freq source = RS-485). The three writeable command registers are: 8192 = command word (run/stop/direction), 8193 = frequency reference (Hz × 100), 8194 = optional fault reset.
